nombre: Baya Voce | Relationship Repair Expert | Columbia University
bio:
If you want a healthy relationship, repair is non-negotiable. I help you learn how.
Supervision w/ Esther Perel
Couples Research @columbia @maps__org
descripcion:
Waiting for someone to change before you let yourself heal is its own kind of trap. The resentment lives in every moment since where you stayed silent, managed their reaction, and left your own needs sitting on the floor.

Your voice might shake and it might be really hard, but the alternative is hard too, and we can do hard things (thanks for that one, @glennondoyle).

#boundaries #selfawareness #relationships

nombre: Baya Voce | Relationship Repair Expert | Columbia University
bio:
If you want a healthy relationship, repair is non-negotiable. I help you learn how.
Supervision w/ Esther Perel
Couples Research @columbia @maps__org
descripcion:
69% of relationship problems never get solved. The question was never how to fix them, it’s whether you can live with them.

Which unsolvable problem are you currently navigating? 👇

#RelationshipAdvice #LongTermRelationship

nombre: Baya Voce | Relationship Repair Expert | Columbia University
bio:
If you want a healthy relationship, repair is non-negotiable. I help you learn how.
Supervision w/ Esther Perel
Couples Research @columbia @maps__org
descripcion:
You can have all the “right” tools and still feel like you’re barely holding it together because you’re trying to show up for hard conversations on an empty tank. We talk a lot about emotional skills, but not enough about the basics that actually support them: sleep, energy, hormones, nourishment. 

It’s a lot easier to stay grounded when your body isn’t already struggling behind the scenes.

Use code BAYA25 for a discount off your membership: https://functionhealth.com/bayavoce

In collaboration with Function Health.

#mentalhealth #selfregulation #wellness

nombre: Baya Voce | Relationship Repair Expert | Columbia University

bio:
Helping you unlock your potential through masterful communication 🚀
Free 2 Hour Training 👇
descripcion:
Please NEVER do this! 

If you want to be a better listener, stop giving advice. I know it's a natural instinct for many of us.

But sometimes what people need is someone who can just be there, understand what they’re feeling, and listen without trying to fix it.

Do you have a friend who's as insensitive as me in this skit? 😬

bio:

descripcion:
What do you think of this gesture?

Comment ‘YouTube’ for the full body language breakdown of things you should do and things you should avoid!

bio:
Helping you unlock your potential through masterful communication 🚀
Free 2 Hour Training 👇
descripcion:
Avoid using this hand gesture!

Comment 'youtube' for the full video breakdown.

This is called the Steeple (or I call it the Mr Burns) and it makes you come across a bit "schemey" and dominant.

I see many speakers default to this hand gesture when speaking to an audience, and when something becomes a default, it becomes non-functional. 

Use it occasionally for effect but don't get stuck in it!

#bodylanguage #communicationskills

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
The fastest way to kill momentum is to keep measuring your life against someone else’s highlight reel.

You can’t build confidence while constantly checking who’s ahead of you. You can’t hear your own ideas when your brain is full of everyone else’s timeline, numbers, relationships, career moves, body, house, success. 

Comparison doesn’t make you better. It makes you distracted.

Most people you envy are just further along in a process you can’t see. They’ve failed privately more times than you’ve probably watched publicly. And meanwhile, while you’re busy wondering why you’re not there yet, you’re missing the fact that you’re already becoming someone you once hoped to be.

The people who actually succeed long term usually aren’t obsessed with competing. They’re obsessed with improving. 

Quietly. Repeatedly. Consistently.

Your path was never supposed to look identical to someone else’s. Different timing does not mean failure. Different strengths do not mean less value.

Comparison convinces you to stop before your life has even had the chance to compound.
What’s something you’ve achieved lately that a past version of you would be proud of?

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
Your teen says, “Nothing happened.”

Their face says otherwise. 😅

On @jenniferhudsonshow I broke down the subtle body language and behavioral cues that can reveal when a teen isn’t telling the whole truth...and why catching the lie isn’t always the goal.

The real skill? Creating enough trust that they tell you the truth in the first place.

Have you ever instantly known your teen was hiding something? 👀

#ParentingTips #BodyLanguage #CommunicationSkills #TeenParenting #JenniferHudsonShow

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
For years, I got lost in metrics.

Views. Followers. Revenue. Opportunities. Growth. Achievement. I kept chasing the next number, convinced that once I hit a certain milestone, I'd finally feel successful.

The problem was that every time I reached a goal, the target moved. There was always another benchmark, another comparison, another reason to feel like I wasn't quite there yet.

It was exhausting.

The shift that changed everything was surprisingly simple: I stopped asking, "How am I doing?" and started asking, "How can I help?"

Now, my favorite metric is whether I was useful.

Did I share something that made someone's life easier? Did I help someone navigate a challenge? Did I give a person the confidence, information, or encouragement they needed to take the next step?

Ironically, focusing less on myself and more on being helpful brought me more fulfillment than any metric ever did.

Numbers can tell you how much attention you're getting. They can't tell you whether you're making a difference.

These days, my definition of success is simple: leave people better than you found them.

What's a metric you've stopped caring about as you've gotten older?

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
Being an introvert is not a bad thing.

Somewhere along the way, a lot of introverts got the message that they should be more outgoing, more social, more talkative, more extroverted.
But you’re not a broken extrovert.

You’re not an extrovert who needs more confidence.

You’re not an extrovert who hasn’t come out of their shell yet.

You’re an introvert.

And when you stop trying to be someone else, you can start building confidence, connection, and success in a way that actually works for you.

Comment INTROVERT and I’ll send you my training on how to harness your personality as a strength.

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
The secret formula to a great presentation is finding the balance between inspiration and information, because no matter how smart, accomplished, or knowledgeable you are, people will stop listening the second they feel like they’re being talked at instead of spoken to. 

PS - if you like tips like this this is just a SAMPLE of what you can expect from my coaching cohort. Comment COACH if you'd like access to our exclusive coaching program, now! 

The presentations that actually leave an impact are the ones that teach people something valuable while also making them feel curious, inspired, challenged, excited, or understood in the process. Facts and data might earn attention for a moment, but emotion is what makes people remember you afterward. 

The best presenters understand that their job isn’t just to transfer information onto a slide deck — it’s to create connection, shift perspective, and make an audience genuinely care about the message they’re hearing.

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
One hand gesture can instantly make you look more confident.

It’s called the steeple. 👆

When someone lightly touches their fingertips together, creating a “church steeple” shape, it often signals confidence, certainty, and authority.

On @jenniferhudsonshow I shared why this is one of the most powerful nonverbal cues in body language. 

The key? Use it naturally. Forced confidence looks forced.

Your body speaks before you do.

Have you ever noticed someone using the steeple in a meeting, interview, or negotiation? 👀

#BodyLanguage #ConfidenceTips #CommunicationSkills #JenniferHudsonShow #Leadership

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
For years, I thought being “bad at networking” was going to ruin my career.

I hated small talk. Hated walking into rooms full of strangers. Hated trying to sound impressive. I thought success belonged to the loudest person in the room, the smoothest talker, the person who could magically turn every conversation into an opportunity.
Meanwhile, I was overthinking every interaction and replaying conversations in my head for hours afterward.

What changed everything was realizing people don’t actually connect through perfect small talk. They connect through feeling seen. Through curiosity.

Through real conversations. Through remembering details. Through making someone feel comfortable instead of trying to perform.

The second I stopped trying to “network” and started trying to genuinely understand people, everything changed. My career grew faster.

My relationships became deeper. Opportunities came from authenticity instead of pretending to be someone I wasn’t.

Turns out, being bad at surface-level conversation wasn’t the thing holding me back. Believing I had to become someone else to succeed was.

Comment CONVERSATION and I’ll send you the pre-order link to my book on creating deeper connections in every area of your life.

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
THESE Are Biggest Interview Mistakes YOU’RE MAKING!

“I don’t understand. I thought the interview went really well.”

Then I ask a few questions.

Did you actually answer the question they asked?

Did you give specific examples?
Did you explain the impact of your work?
Did you ask thoughtful questions back?

Usually the answer is... not really.
Most people think interviews are about proving they’re qualified.

They’re about helping someone picture what it’s like to work with you.
If your answers are too long, too vague, overly rehearsed, or sound like a résumé being read out loud, you’re making it harder for the interviewer to see your value.

The frustrating part is that these mistakes are often invisible to the person making them.
That’s why so many talented people leave interviews feeling confident... and never get the call.

The good news? Interviewing is a skill. And skills can be learned.

Comment INTERVIEW and I’ll send you training on how to answer questions confidently, communicate your value, and stand out from the competition.

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
One of the reasons social skills feel so overwhelming is that people try to learn all of them at once.

They think they need to become more confident, more charismatic, better at small talk, better at networking, better at public speaking, and better at reading body language... all at the same time.

No wonder it feels impossible.

If you want someone to show you exactly what to work on first, comment **COACH** and we'll send you the details about our coaching program.

The people who become socially skilled rarely transform overnight. They focus on micro social skills.

They practice making eye contact for one extra second. They learn how to ask a better follow-up question.

They work on remembering names. They get comfortable introducing themselves to one new person. They learn how to enter a group conversation without feeling awkward.

Each skill seems small on its own, but together they create massive change.

The goal isn't to become a completely different person. The goal is to get comfortable with one new social behavior at a time until it becomes part of who you are.

Confidence is usually the result of competence.

And competence is built through small, repeatable wins.

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
I always knew I wanted to help people. I just didn't always know how.
For years I felt like everyone else got a manual for human connection that I somehow missed. Conversations felt awkward and I constantly felt like people were mad at me.
Then I started learning the actual science of people skills — and it changed my life. Not slowly. Not vaguely. It rewired how I walked into a room, how I connected, how I was seen. And the wildest part? How fast it happened. I watched my whole life shift once I had the right tools, and I've spent 18 years since proving these frameworks work — with Fortune 500 leaders, educators, therapists, and entrepreneurs. In the last two years the demand for soft skills has increased exponentially. Not only for individuals who want to learn how to communicate with confidence but also inside companies. Trainings, workshops and lunch and learns on communication are in demand in the Age of AI as it is the best form of career insurance.
That's why I built the People Coach Certification. So I could hand these tools to the people who are already out there helping others — coaches, managers, teachers, therapists, facilitators. I'm looking for 25 people for Cohort 2. We now have 30 certified coaches from Cohort 1 and I am ready for our next group! I'm looking for 2 types of people: 1. Intrapreneurs - people in corporate looking to do communication coaching in their company or in their role. 2. Entrepreneurs - life coaches, therapists, social workers, counselors, and executive coaches who want to bring this curriculum into their current practice.
This is a rigorous, science-backed certification: our PEOPLE framework, the D3 Lesson Loop™, proprietary assessments, done-for-you workshop kits, live sessions with me, and a private community of serious professionals.
For this reason this is application only. I'm looking for folks with the motivation to use communication as a force for good, alignment with our mission, and the drive to actually do the work. When you're accepted, you'll know you're standing among a high-caliber group who take this as seriously as you do.
Our first cohort just produced 30 certified People Coaches — and watching them go out

bio:
Harvard Instructor & Behavioral Researcher
🗣️ Helping you master communication
Author, Captivate & Cues | DM “QUIZ” for Your Blind Spot
descripcion:
Like behavior tips like this? Comment COACH for access to my signature coaching program with much more on how to put people at ease! 

One of the easiest ways to make someone instantly light up in conversation: pay attention to their phone screen.

People put what they love most right in front of them — their dog, their kids, a favorite place, a hobby, a memory they never want to forget. If you notice it and bring it up naturally, you skip surface-level small talk and go straight into something they actually care about.

“Wait, is that your golden retriever?”
“Where was that photo taken?”
“Your kid is adorable — how old are they?”

The fastest way to be interesting is to make people feel seen.

bio:
Lawyer → VP → Founder
Your team isn't the problem. Your leadership system is.
I help $1M+ founders/CEOs lead without being the bottleneck.
Start Here👇
descripcion:
Your team isn’t slow. They’re not disengaged. They’re not the wrong hires.

They just don’t feel safe making the call without you.

Here’s what that looks like in practice:

1. They ask you questions they already know the answer to.

2. They send messages that start with “just wanted to check with you before I...”

3. A decision that should take 10 minutes waits until your next 1:1.

4. They present options instead of a recommendation.

5. They cc you on emails that don’t require your input.

6. They redo work after your feedback even when the first version was fine.

7. They go quiet in meetings when the topic is outside their explicit lane.

8. They tell you what happened after it’s already done, never before.

9. When something goes sideways, the first call is to you.

10. They say “I wasn’t sure if I should” more than “I handled it.”

11. Your calendar is full of conversations that exist because your team doesn’t trust their own judgment yet.

This isn’t a people problem. It’s a missing layer in how your business is built.

I wrote the full breakdown on Substack, what’s creating this dynamic and the 4 things that change it.

Comment SUBSTACK and I’ll DM you the link.

#leadershipcommunication

descripcion:
Resourcefulness is one of 3 values we have at my company.
Which means when something goes sideways — and it always does — my team doesn’t stop and wait for me.

They find a way.

Because we defined what resourcefulness actually looks like in my business. 

We made it a standard, not just a word on a slide. And I built safety so they could act on it without my sign-off.

By constantly reinforcing the value, recognizing the people who practice it, and giving them the safety to execute, I can walk away knowing the work will get done the way I need it to be done.

That’s not hoping they get it. That’s infrastructure.

#leadershipcommunication

The worst managers in the world aren’t the inexperienced ones.

They’re the ones who can’t let go.

They don’t build leaders. They build minions.

They confuse being feared with being respected.

And the second somebody on their team becomes truly capable, they feel threatened — not proud.
